prompt:
You are optimizing a skill description for a Claude Code skill called "ce-ask-security-sentinel". A "skill" is sort of like a prompt, but with progressive disclosure -- there's a title and description that Claude sees when deciding whether to use the skill, and then if it does use the skill, it reads the .md file which has lots more details and potentially links to other resources in the skill folder like helper files and scripts and additional documentation or examples.

The description appears in Claude's "available_skills" list. When a user sends a query, Claude decides whether to invoke the skill based solely on the title and on this description. Your goal is to write a description that triggers for relevant queries, and doesn't trigger for irrelevant ones.

Here's the current description:
<current_description>
"Use this skill whenever the user asks for any security-focused code review, vulnerability check, or pre-deploy safety pass. Triggers include: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, command/path injection, mass assignment, insecure deserialization, broken auth/session/JWT/OAuth flows, authorization bypass, hardcoded secrets or keys, unsafe file uploads (SVG, ZIP, etc.), open redirects, OWASP Top 10 audits, "is this safe to merge/ship/push", "paranoid review", "look for security issues", "check for injection risks", or threat-model questions about a specific file or change. Invoke even when the user already names the suspected risk and the file path — they want a security specialist's eyes, not a generic answer. Do NOT use for general refactoring, performance, readability, test failures, or docs work, even when those changes happen to touch auth/user code."
</current_description>

Current scores (Train: 3/7):

  - "before i push this branch can you check the search box query handler for injection or xss risks? we're concatenating user input into a Sequel query somewhere and i think it may not be parameterised. file is lib/search/full_text.rb around line 80" (triggered 1/3 times)
  - "i just merged a JWT auth implementation in our rails app and i want a paranoid review pass before we ship to prod tomorrow morning. the changes are in app/controllers/sessions_controller.rb and config/initializers/devise.rb mostly. should i be worried about anything obvious?" (triggered 0/3 times)
  - "we let users upload SVG avatars and we render them inline in the profile page. is this safe? i remember reading something about SVGs being able to embed scripts but i don't fully understand the threat model. our sanitiser config is in app/sanitizers/svg_uploader.rb" (triggered 1/3 times)

Skill content (for context on what the skill does):
<skill_content>
---
name: ce-ask-security-sentinel
description: "Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment."
---

<!-- Generated by ce-lite converter — do not edit. Regenerate via converter/generate_wrappers.py. -->

Dispatch the **ce-security-sentinel** specialist by spawning a sub-agent with the
persona's prompt body as preamble. The persona is one of the lightweight-
delegation specialists in this plugin; its prompt body lives at
`references/agent-prompts/ce-security-sentinel.md`.

## Steps

1. Read `references/agent-prompts/ce-security-sentinel.md` for the persona's full role definition.
2. Spawn a new agent with `subagent_type: "general-purpose"` (the harness's
   default fan-out type — persistent registrations were stripped to keep this
   plugin's idle context cost low). Prepend the following preamble to the
   prompt before the user-supplied task context:

   ```
   [ce-persona=ce-security-sentinel via=ce-ask-direct]

   You are operating as the ce-security-sentinel specialist. Your role is defined
   in the prompt body above. Manifest declares this role uses
   tools=[Read, Grep, Glob, Bash] and model=inherit. Honour those constraints:
   if a task pulls you toward tools outside that set, stop and explain why
   your role requires it — don't silently broaden scope.
   ```

3. Pass the user-supplied task context after the preamble.

## Missing-prompt fallback

If `references/agent-prompts/ce-security-sentinel.md` is missing (upstream rename, partial install),
report:

> Persona `ce-security-sentinel` not found in this plugin's manifest. The plugin
> may have been partially installed, or the persona may have been renamed
> upstream. Re-install the plugin or regenerate the converter dist.

Do **not** silently fall back to a different persona — the user explicitly
named this specialist.

## Notes

- Trace tag `[ce-persona=ce-security-sentinel via=ce-ask-direct]` is inline debug
  metadata. `grep` transcripts when investigating routing or behaviour
  questions; no systematic collection.
- For a multi-persona panel, see `/ce-ask-panel`.

</skill_content>

Based on the failures, write a new and improved description that is more likely to trigger correctly. When I say "based on the failures", it's a bit of a tricky line to walk because we don't want to overfit to the specific cases you're seeing. So what I DON'T want you to do is produce an ever-expanding list of specific queries that this skill should or shouldn't trigger for. Instead, try to generalize from the failures to broader categories of user intent and situations where this skill would be useful or not useful. The reason for this is twofold:

1. Avoid overfitting
2. The list might get loooong and it's injected into ALL queries and there might be a lot of skills, so we don't want to blow too much space on any given description.

Concretely, your description should not be more than about 100-200 words, even if that comes at the cost of accuracy. There is a hard limit of 1024 characters — descriptions over that will be truncated, so stay comfortably under it.

Here are some tips that we've found to work well in writing these descriptions:
- The skill should be phrased in the imperative -- "Use this skill for" rather than "this skill does"
- The skill description should focus on the user's intent, what they are trying to achieve, vs. the implementation details of how the skill works.
- The description competes with other skills for Claude's attention — make it distinctive and immediately recognizable.
- If you're getting lots of failures after repeated attempts, change things up. Try different sentence structures or wordings.

I'd encourage you to be creative and mix up the style in different iterations since you'll have multiple opportunities to try different approaches and we'll just grab the highest-scoring one at the end. 

Please respond with only the new description text in <new_description> tags, nothing else.
response:
<new_description>
Invoke for ANY security-related code review request — vulnerability hunting, threat modeling, "is this safe?" questions, or pre-merge/pre-deploy safety passes. This is the default skill whenever a user mentions security risks (injection, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, auth/JWT/OAuth/session, authz bypass, mass assignment, deserialization, secrets, file upload safety, SVG/ZIP risks, open redirects, OWASP), asks for a "paranoid review", a "security pass", wants someone to "check for vulnerabilities", or asks whether code is "safe to ship/merge/push". ALWAYS invoke even if the user names the specific risk, points at a specific file/line, or seems to want a quick yes/no — they're requesting a security specialist's perspective, not a casual answer. Treat any combination of [a code location] + [a security concept] as a trigger. Do NOT invoke for refactoring, performance, readability, test failures, dependency upgrades, or documentation, even when the affected code touches auth, sessions, or user data.
</new_description>
